walkthrough.title: Place the Star Cluster Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Pulsar 2849 set up. First, grab the big star cluster board and place it in the center of the table. This board is double-sided. One side has dead end locations, which changes tactics a bit. For your first game, use the side without dead ends. It is marked with a special icon on the 0 points space. This is where all the pulsars, planetary systems, and flight paths live.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Dice Board
walkthrough.text: Now find the double-sided dice board. This board depends on your player count. Use one side for a 4-player game and the other side for a 2- or 3-player game. Place it against the star cluster board so it is easy to reach. This is where dice will be placed after rolling and where players will track initiative and engineering.

walkthrough.title: Gather the Dice
walkthrough.text: Grab the dice. In a 4-player game, you use all 9 silver dice plus the 1 red die. In a 2- or 3-player game, use only 7 silver dice and the red die. Put the extra silver dice back in the box if you do not need them. Place all the dice in the black hole area in the center of the dice board. The red die stays there too for now. Keep the median marker nearby.

walkthrough.title: Deal Planetary System Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 17 planetary system tiles. Turn them all face down and mix them up. Deal 16 of them face-down onto the planetary system locations on the star cluster board. Return the remaining tile to the box without looking at it. These tiles will be revealed when players fly their ships to those locations.

walkthrough.title: Place the Gyrodyne Board and Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place the gyrodyne board against the star cluster board. Now separate the 30 gyrodyne tiles by type. There are three sizes: small, medium, and large, with 10 of each. Stack each group in its designated space on the gyrodyne board. Then make three stacks of construction award tiles: each stack has a 7-point tile on top and a 4-point tile underneath. Place these stacks on the indicated spaces of the gyrodyne board.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Die Modifiers Board
walkthrough.text: Place the die modifiers board against the star cluster board. Stack the plus-minus die modifier tokens on their corresponding space. You will give one to each player during player setup. Stack the plus-two die modifier tokens on their space as well. Players can earn these during the game to boost their dice values.

walkthrough.title: Choose and Place Technology Boards
walkthrough.text: Technologies come in three categories: I, II, and III. You need one board for each category. For your first game, use boards AI, AII, and AIII. In a standard game, you can choose each category's board randomly, like DI, AII, and CIII. Place them against the star cluster board in order, with board I on the inside closest to the board and board III on the outside. Set the time marker to point at the innermost row of technologies.

walkthrough.title: Choose and Place Goal Tiles
walkthrough.text: There are 6 double-sided goal tiles. For your first game, use the three tiles marked with a special beginner symbol and place them with the marked side up. In a standard game, choose 3 at random, play with a random side up, and return the unused 3 to the box. Place the chosen goal tiles against the star cluster board.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Transmitter Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 24 transmitter tiles and group them by their letters: A, B, and C. Stack each group with the lettered side up and shuffle within each group. Then make one big stack with all the Cs on the bottom, all Bs in the middle, and all As on top. Deal the top 3 transmitters face up beside the stack so all players can see the silver dice printed on them. These are the transmitters available for this round.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Engineering Cubes and Exploration Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place the engineering cubes and four-cube tokens in a shared supply where everyone can reach them. Each four-cube token represents 4 engineering cubes, and players can make change freely. Shuffle the exploration bonus tiles face-down into a stack within reach of all players.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses a color and takes the following: 3 rocket figures (only 2 are used in a 3- or 4-player game), 6 pulsar claim rings, 1 hundred-point tile, 1 plus-minus die modifier token, and a bunch of plastic tokens in their color. Each player also takes a quick reference sheet. For your first game, leave the HQ boards in the box. In a standard game, each player receives a randomly selected HQ board and decides which side to use before the first dice roll.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Play Order and Tracks
walkthrough.text: Randomly determine a play order. Place one rocket figure of each player's color on the dice board's play-order spaces in that order. Each player also places two of their tokens on the dice board as counters: one on the initiative track starting space and one on the engineering track starting space. These counters are stacked in reverse play order, with the first player's counter on the bottom and the last player's counter on top.

walkthrough.title: Set Starting Scores and Choose Entry Gates
walkthrough.text: Place each player's token around the edge of the star cluster board as a score counter. The starting player begins at 5 points, the second player at 6, the third at 7, and the fourth at 8. These extra points compensate for the disadvantage of going later in the first round. Place each player's hundred-point tile near space 0. Then, beginning with the last player and going in reverse play order, each player chooses one unoccupied entry gate on the star cluster board and places a rocket figure there. This is your survey ship's starting location.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: That is it for setup. You should have the star cluster board in the center surrounded by the dice board, gyrodyne board, die modifiers board, technology boards, and goal tiles. The transmitter stack is nearby with 3 face-up tiles. Each player has a rocket on a play-order space, a rocket on an entry gate, two counters on the tracks, and their personal supply of rings, tokens, and a die modifier. Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Double-sided: use the side without dead ends for your first game
Place centrally where all players can reach

bullets: Use the correct side for your player count (4-player vs 2/3-player)
Place against the star cluster board

bullets: 4 players: 9 silver dice + 1 red die
2-3 players: 7 silver dice + 1 red die (leave 2 silver in box)
Place all dice in the black hole on the dice board

Return remaining tile to box unseen

Place score token on star cluster board edge
In reverse order, each player picks an entry gate and places survey ship rocket

summary.title: Ready to Play
bullets: Star cluster board center, surrounded by all auxiliary boards
3 face-up transmitters beside the stack
Each player: rocket on play-order space, rocket on entry gate, 2 track counters, personal supply

walkthrough.title: The Eight Rounds
walkthrough.text: Pulsar 2849 is played over 8 rounds. Each round has three phases in order: the Dice Phase, the Action Phase, and the Production Phase. During the Dice Phase you roll dice and everyone drafts two of them. During the Action Phase you spend those dice on actions to build your space corporation. During the Production Phase you score points from your spinning gyrodynes and online transmitters, gain engineering cubes, and set up for the next round. After 8 rounds, you do final scoring and the player with the most points wins.

walkthrough.title: Dice Phase: Rolling and Finding the Median
walkthrough.text: The starting player rolls all the silver dice and places each one on the dice board space matching its value. Now you need to find the median. The median is the die in the middle when you line them all up in order. Place the median marker on that die space. If there are more dice to the left of the marker, move it one space left. If more to the right, move it one space right. If equal on both sides, the marker stays on a die space. The median determines whether choosing a die moves your tracker left or right.

walkthrough.title: Dice Phase: Drafting Dice
walkthrough.text: Starting with the first player, each player takes one die from the dice board. When you take a die, you must move one of your two counters, either initiative or engineering. If you took a die from a space to the right of the median, move your chosen counter right. If from the left, move it left. The number of spaces you move equals the distance from the median marker to your die. If your die is on the median space, you do not move at all. After everyone takes one die, you go again in reverse order for the second die. One silver die will be left over, and it can be copied as a bonus die during the Action Phase by spending 4 engineering cubes.

walkthrough.title: The Tracker Tension
walkthrough.text: Here is the key tension of the dice draft. High-value dice are powerful but they sit to the right of the median, so taking them pushes your counter to the right. Being far right on the initiative track means you go later next round and might lose points as a penalty. Being far right on the engineering track means fewer engineering cubes and possible point penalties too. Low-value dice push you left, which is good for track position but might not give you the die value you need. The median die itself is free, no movement at all. Every dice draft is a trade-off between the die you want and the track cost.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase: Overview
walkthrough.text: During the Action Phase, each player takes one turn in play order. On your turn, you spend your two drafted dice (and possibly a bonus die) on actions. You can do the same action twice if you have the right dice. Actions include: flying your survey ship, taking a gyrodyne tile, starting a gyrodyne spinning, taking or working on a transmitter, patenting a technology, completing an HQ project, or buying a die modifier token. You can also start construction of a gyrodyne on a claimed pulsar for free at any point during your turn.

walkthrough.title: Action: Flying Your Survey Ship
walkthrough.text: To fly your survey ship, spend one die of any value. The value determines how many flight segments you travel. You must fly exactly that far, no more, no less. Your ship moves along flight segments connecting locations: jump gates, pulsars, and planetary systems. You cannot retrace any segment within the same flight. When you pass through a planetary system, flip it face up if it is still hidden. If you just pass through, you establish a station on a lifeless planet if available, or a blue planet if no lifeless one exists. You get no exploration bonus for passing through. If your flight ends at a planetary system, you place your token on a blue planet if available and get an exploration bonus. If no blue planet is available, you go on a lifeless planet with no bonus. If your flight ends at an unclaimed pulsar, you claim it by placing one of your 6 rings around it.

walkthrough.title: Exploration Bonuses
walkthrough.text: When your flight ends at a planetary system and you establish a station on a blue planet, you get an exploration bonus. You can either take the bonus depicted on the planetary system tile, or draw a random exploration bonus tile from the stack. Most exploration bonus tiles give you points and are discarded immediately. Some give die modifiers, gyrodyne tiles, or other benefits. A few are kept for end-game scoring. On the dead ends side of the board, claiming a dead end pulsar or establishing a station in a dead end system earns you an extra exploration bonus tile draw.

walkthrough.title: Action: Taking a Gyrodyne Tile
walkthrough.text: To take a gyrodyne tile, spend a die matching the required value: a 1 for a small gyrodyne, a 2 for a medium, or a 4 for a large. The cost is printed on the gyrodyne board. You can also get gyrodyne tiles as bonuses from planetary systems, HQ projects, or technologies, which is often more efficient. You can take a gyrodyne tile even if you have not claimed a pulsar yet. Just keep it in front of you until you are ready to place it.

walkthrough.title: Developing Pulsars: Construction and Spinning
walkthrough.text: Once you have a claimed pulsar and a gyrodyne tile, you can place the tile on the pulsar for free at any point during your turn. The tile goes construction-side up. A pulsar can have at most one gyrodyne, and once placed, you cannot swap it. To complete the gyrodyne and start it spinning, spend the die shown on the tile: a 3 for small, a 5 for medium, or a 6 for large. Flip it over. It is now spinning and will produce points every Production Phase for the rest of the game. The points it produces equal its face value plus the bonus from the current technology row.

walkthrough.title: Gyrodyne Construction Awards
walkthrough.text: There is a race for construction awards. The first player to have at least two gyrodynes of the same size on the board, whether under construction or spinning, takes the 7-point award tile for that size. The second player to meet the same criteria takes the 4-point tile. Awards are available for each of the three sizes independently, so you could win awards for multiple sizes. If you get four of the same size on the board before anyone else has more than one, you take both the 7-point and 4-point tiles.

walkthrough.title: Action: Building Transmitters
walkthrough.text: Each round, 3 transmitter tiles are available. To take one, spend a die matching any of the dice printed on the tile and cover that die icon with your token. Place the transmitter in front of you. Your second and later transmitters can be linked end-to-end to form arrays using the half-die symbols on their edges. When you take a new transmitter, you must immediately decide whether to link it to an existing one or start a new array. Once linked, transmitters cannot be unlinked. To complete a transmitter, you need to pay each die cost shown on it. One was already paid when you took it. Pay the remaining costs over subsequent turns by spending dice and covering the icons. Once fully paid, flip it over. It is now online.

walkthrough.title: Online Transmitters and Bonus Dice
walkthrough.text: When a transmitter goes online, it may give you an immediate benefit as shown on the front side. Then flip it. The back side shows a production benefit you gain every Production Phase going forward, such as points or engineering cubes. Here is the exciting part: when two linked transmitters are both online, they form a red bonus die at the junction. You can use this die as your bonus die this turn. If you do not use it now, it is wasted. Remember, you can only use one bonus die per turn.

walkthrough.title: Action: Patenting Technologies
walkthrough.text: Technology boards show rows of technologies that become available as the game progresses. In round 1, only the innermost row is available. Each round, the time marker moves outward to unlock a new row, and all previous rows stay in play. To patent a technology, spend a die matching its cost and place your token on it. Each technology can be patented by at most two players. Technologies with one die icon can only be patented by one player per round, but a second player can patent it in a later round. Technologies with two die icons can be patented by two players even in the same round. In a 2-player game, one-die-icon technologies can only be patented by one player total.

walkthrough.title: Technology Effects
walkthrough.text: Technologies come in different colors indicating when they take effect. Some give immediate bonuses when patented, like points, gyrodyne tiles, or die modifiers. Some give you ongoing special abilities for the rest of the game, like modifying flight rules or scoring bonus points for certain actions. Green technologies give you points at the end of every Production Phase. Purple technologies score at the end of the game. Technologies are very powerful and can shape your entire strategy.

walkthrough.title: Action: HQ Projects
walkthrough.text: In a standard game, each player has a unique HQ board with a pyramid of projects. Only the bottom row projects are available at the start. Complete a project by spending the indicated die and marking it with your token. Completing two bottom-row projects unlocks the second row, and so on up to the top project. Projects give immediate benefits similar to planetary exploration bonuses. Some projects unlock gate runs: when you fly your ship, you can announce a gate run and score points for each jump gate of the indicated color you visit during that flight. Each gate run project can be used once. Skip HQ boards for your first game.

walkthrough.title: Die Modifiers and Bonus Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with one plus-minus die modifier token. This lets you change a die value by 1, turning a 4 into a 3 or 5, for example. Plus-two tokens add 2 to a die. Tokens cannot be combined with each other, but they can be combined with technology abilities. You can buy a plus-minus token by spending a 1 or 2 die, or a plus-two token by spending a 2 die. For bonus dice: you can spend 4 engineering cubes to copy the leftover die from the draft. You can also gain bonus dice from transmitter links, certain technologies, and some exploration bonuses. Only one bonus die per turn.

walkthrough.title: Production Phase
walkthrough.text: After everyone has taken their Action Phase turn, the Production Phase happens in this order: First, determine new play order by rearranging rockets based on initiative track position, with the leftmost player going first. Second, hand out engineering cubes based on engineering track position. Third, take initiative and engineering penalties: counters on the -1 or -2 spaces lose that many points. Fourth, score online transmitters for their production benefits. Fifth, score all spinning gyrodynes. They each produce their face value plus the row bonus shown by the time marker. Sixth, score green technologies. Seventh, set up for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Setting Up for the Next Round
walkthrough.text: Discard any untaken transmitters and deal 3 new ones from the stack. For each technology patented for the first time this round, move its token down to open a second patent slot. Move the time marker outward to unlock the next row of technologies. Then start the next round with a fresh dice roll. After round 8, proceed to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After round 8, score the following in order: First, score purple patent end-game bonuses. Second, score goal tiles. If you achieve a goal, you score its base points and can optionally buy additional bonuses with engineering cubes. Third, score 1 point per claimed pulsar that has no gyrodyne or has a gyrodyne still under construction. Fourth, score 1 point per gyrodyne tile still under construction or not yet placed. Fifth, score 1 point per pair of engineering cubes you have left. Sixth, score initiative track position: 7 points for first, 4 for second, 2 for third in a 4-player game, no third-place points in a 3-player game. Seventh, score points for total stations in planetary systems, up to 50 for 13 stations plus 3 per station beyond 13. The player with the most points wins. Break ties by initiative track position.

summary.title: The Eight Rounds
bullets: Game = 8 rounds, each with Dice Phase, Action Phase, Production Phase
Dice Phase: roll and draft 2 dice each, affecting track positions
Action Phase: spend dice on actions; Production Phase: score and reset

summary.title: Dice Phase: Rolling and Finding the Median
bullets: First player rolls all silver dice, places on matching board spaces
Find median die (middle of lineup), place median marker
If more dice left of marker, shift left; more right, shift right; equal, stays

summary.title: Dice Phase: Drafting Dice
bullets: Players draft 1 die each in play order, then 1 more in reverse order
Taking die right of median = move chosen counter right (bad); left = move left (good)
Distance from median = number of spaces moved; on median = free, no movement
1 leftover die can be copied as bonus die for 4 engineering cubes

summary.title: The Tracker Tension
bullets: Initiative track: far left = go first + end-game points; far right = go last + penalties
Engineering track: far left = more cubes; far right = fewer cubes + penalties
High dice are powerful but push you right; low dice push you left
Median die is free (no track movement) but may not be the value you need

summary.title: Action Phase: Overview
bullets: Each player takes one turn in play order, spending 2 dice + up to 1 bonus die
Actions: fly ship, take gyrodyne, spin gyrodyne, take/build transmitter, patent technology, HQ project, buy modifier
Same action can be done twice if you have the right dice

summary.title: Action: Flying Your Survey Ship
bullets: Spend any die; value = exact number of flight segments
Cannot retrace a segment within one flight
Pass through system: station on lifeless planet (or blue if no lifeless), no bonus
End at system: station on blue planet + exploration bonus; end at pulsar: claim it

summary.title: Exploration Bonuses
bullets: End flight at system + blue planet = take system's bonus OR draw random tile
Most tiles give points and are discarded; some give modifiers, gyrodynes, etc.
Dead end system/pulsar earns an extra exploration tile draw

summary.title: Action: Taking a Gyrodyne Tile
bullets: Spend die: 1 = small, 2 = medium, 4 = large gyrodyne
Can also get gyrodynes from planet bonuses, HQ projects, technologies
Can take a tile even without a claimed pulsar; hold it until ready

summary.title: Developing Pulsars: Construction and Spinning
bullets: Place gyrodyne on claimed pulsar for free (construction side up)
Spend die to flip and start spinning: 3 = small, 5 = medium, 6 = large
Spinning gyrodyne produces points every Production Phase (face value + row bonus)
Max 1 gyrodyne per pulsar; cannot swap once placed

summary.title: Gyrodyne Construction Awards
bullets: First player with 2+ same-size gyrodynes on board = 7-point award
Second player to meet same criteria = 4-point award
Awards exist for each size independently (small, medium, large)
Get 4 of same size before anyone has 2 = take both 7 and 4 point tiles

summary.title: Action: Building Transmitters
bullets: 3 transmitters available each round; spend matching die to take one
Link transmitters end-to-end in arrays; must decide linkage immediately
Pay remaining die costs over future turns to complete
Once all costs paid: flip to online, gain immediate benefit

summary.title: Online Transmitters and Bonus Dice
bullets: Online transmitters provide production benefits every round
Two linked online transmitters form a red bonus die at junction
Bonus die can be used this turn only; max 1 bonus die per turn
Some transmitters produce points, cubes, or let you buy points

summary.title: Action: Patenting Technologies
bullets: New row unlocks each round; all previous rows remain available
Spend die matching cost, place token; max 2 players per technology
One-die-icon tech: 1 player per round (2nd player next round or later)
Two-die-icon tech: 2 players can patent in same round

summary.title: Technology Effects
bullets: Immediate bonuses: points, gyrodynes, die modifiers
Ongoing abilities: modify flights, score for specific actions
Green technologies: score every Production Phase
Purple technologies: score at end of game

summary.title: Action: HQ Projects
bullets: Unique player board with pyramid of projects (skip for first game)
Complete by spending indicated die; unlocks higher-tier projects
Some projects unlock gate runs: score points per jump gate visited of a color
Each gate run project usable once per game

summary.title: Die Modifiers and Bonus Dice
bullets: Plus-minus token: change die by +/- 1; Plus-two token: add 2 to die
Buy plus-minus with a 1 or 2 die; buy plus-two with a 2 die
Bonus die sources: leftover die (4 cubes), transmitter links, technologies, planets
Max 1 bonus die per turn; can use die modifiers on bonus dice too

summary.title: Production Phase
bullets: 1. New play order from initiative track (leftmost = first)
2. Engineering cubes based on track position
3. Penalties: -1 or -2 points for counters on right-end penalty spaces
4. Online transmitters produce; 5. Spinning gyrodynes produce; 6. Green tech scores
7. Set up for next round

summary.title: Setting Up for the Next Round
bullets: Discard untaken transmitters, deal 3 new from stack
Move first-patent tokens down to open second slots
Advance time marker to unlock next technology row

summary.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
bullets: 1. Purple patents; 2. Goal tiles (base + buy bonuses with cubes)
3. 1 pt per undeveloped pulsar; 4. 1 pt per unused gyrodyne tile
5. 1 pt per pair of engineering cubes; 6. Initiative: 7/4/2 pts for 1st/2nd/3rd
7. Station points (up to 50 for 13 stations, +3 per extra); tie-break: initiative

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three players: Red, Green, and Blue. Each will make different choices so you can see the major actions in practice. One player will make some classic beginner mistakes along the way. Follow along even if your group is larger or smaller. Let's get into it.

walkthrough.title: The Dice Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: Red is the starting player and rolls the 7 silver dice. They land as: 1, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5, 6. The dice are placed on the dice board on their matching spaces. Now we find the median. Lined up in order, the middle die is the second 3. The median marker goes on the 3 space. There are 3 dice to the left (1, 2, 3) and 3 dice to the right (4, 5, 6), so the marker stays right on the 3 space.

walkthrough.title: Red Drafts First: Grabbing the 6
walkthrough.text: Red wants the 6 to start a large gyrodyne spinning. She takes the 6, which is 3 spaces to the right of the median. She must move one of her counters 3 spaces to the right. She chooses her engineering counter, sacrificing some cubes later to get a powerful action now. Smart trade-off. She puts the 6 die aside as her first action die.

walkthrough.title: Green Drafts: Taking the 3 for Free
walkthrough.text: Green takes a 3 from the median space. Since it is right on the median, Green does not move either counter at all. Zero track cost. Green plans to use this 3 to start a small gyrodyne spinning later. Free dice are always tempting because they cost you nothing on the tracks.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Mistake: Ignoring the Tracks
walkthrough.text: Blue sees the 5 and grabs it, wanting to fly a long survey ship flight. The 5 is 2 spaces right of the median. Blue moves her initiative counter 2 spaces to the right. Then, on the reverse draft, Blue takes the 4, which is 1 space right of the median. She moves her engineering counter 1 space right. Both of Blue's counters are now pushed to the right. She has great dice values, but she has given up play order and engineering cubes for the next round.

walkthrough.title: What Blue Did Wrong
walkthrough.text: Blue grabbed two high-value dice without thinking about track consequences. Taking dice to the right of the median pushes your counters right, which means fewer engineering cubes, possibly going last next round, and potentially losing points from penalty spaces. A better approach would have been to pair a high die with a low die or a median die to balance out the track cost. You do not always need two big dice. One good die and one that helps your track position is often the smarter play.

walkthrough.title: Finishing the Draft
walkthrough.text: In reverse order, Green takes the 2, moving her initiative counter 1 space left, nice. Red takes the 1, moving her initiative counter 2 spaces left, excellent for play order. The remaining die is a 3 sitting on the board. Anyone can copy it during the Action Phase by spending 4 engineering cubes. Now each player has two dice and the Action Phase begins.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn: Flying and Spinning
walkthrough.text: Red goes first. She spends her 1 die to fly her survey ship one segment from her entry gate to a nearby planetary system. The system tile is face down, so she flips it, revealing two planets: one blue and one lifeless. Since her flight ends here, she places her token on the blue planet and takes the exploration bonus. The tile offers two plus-minus die modifier tokens. She takes them. Nice haul. Then Red places a gyrodyne tile she picked up earlier on one of her claimed pulsars for free. She spends her 6 die to start that large gyrodyne spinning. It will produce points every round from now on.

walkthrough.title: Why Red's Turn Was Efficient
walkthrough.text: Red accomplished three things with two dice. She explored a system and got modifier tokens, she placed a gyrodyne on a pulsar for free, and she started that gyrodyne spinning. The exploration bonus gave her resources without costing an extra die. And the spinning gyrodyne will compound points every Production Phase for the rest of the game. Getting a gyrodyne spinning early is one of the best things you can do because the points stack up over all remaining rounds.

walkthrough.title: Green's Turn: Building Transmitters
walkthrough.text: Green takes a transmitter tile from the three available, spending her 2 die to match one of the die costs printed on it. She covers that die icon with her token. The transmitter has one more die cost to pay, so it is not online yet. She will finish it in a future round. Then Green spends her 3 die to start a small gyrodyne spinning on a pulsar she claimed earlier. Two productive actions.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Mistake: Wasting a High Die
walkthrough.text: Blue wants to take a gyrodyne tile, so she spends her 4 die to take a large gyrodyne from the board. That is fine. But then she spends her 5 die to fly her survey ship. She flies 5 segments, passing through two planetary systems and ending at a third. She flips tiles, establishes stations on lifeless planets as she passes through, and ends at the final system on a blue planet for an exploration bonus. Sounds great, right? But here is the problem: Blue passed through two systems without getting exploration bonuses for them, and the long flight used her highest die when a 2 or 3 would have been enough to reach the nearest unclaimed pulsar.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Flights Carefully
walkthrough.text: Flying far is exciting, but it is often wasteful. When you pass through a system, you only place a station on a lifeless planet with no bonus. You only get the exploration bonus when your flight ends at a system with a blue planet available. A shorter, targeted flight ending at a pulsar or a high-value system is usually better than a long scenic tour. Save high-value dice for actions that specifically require high numbers, like spinning medium or large gyrodynes. A 5 die is precious. Using it as mere fuel for a long flight is like burning premium gasoline in a lawnmower.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Second Mistake: Forgetting the Bonus Die
walkthrough.text: After using both dice, Blue remembers there is a leftover 3 on the dice board that can be copied as a bonus die for 4 engineering cubes. But Blue only has 2 engineering cubes because her engineering counter got pushed so far right during the draft. She cannot afford the bonus die. Red, on the other hand, has plenty of cubes and already used a bonus die to buy a modifier token. Blue's aggressive drafting cost her both track position and the bonus die opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Engineering Cubes Are Currency
walkthrough.text: Engineering cubes are more valuable than they look. They let you copy a bonus die for 4 cubes, which effectively gives you a third action each round. They are used to buy end-game goal bonuses. And leftover cubes score 1 point per pair at game end. When you push your engineering counter to the right to draft attractive dice, you lose cubes and might even take point penalties. Always weigh the die value against the cube cost. Sometimes that median die is the best pick because it costs you nothing on any track.

walkthrough.title: The Production Phase
walkthrough.text: Action Phase is done. Now the Production Phase kicks in. First, rearrange the play-order rockets based on initiative track position. Red moved left twice, so she is farthest left and will go first again next round. Blue is farthest right and will go last. Next, hand out engineering cubes based on engineering track position. Red and Green get cubes. Blue gets fewer because she is pushed right. Then check for penalties. Blue has a counter on a -1 penalty space and loses a point. Finally, score spinning gyrodynes. Red's large gyrodyne produces its face value plus the row bonus. Green's small gyrodyne produces as well. Blue has no spinning gyrodynes yet and scores nothing here.

walkthrough.title: Setting Up for Round 2
walkthrough.text: Discard the untaken transmitters and deal 3 new ones. For any technology patented for the first time this round, slide the token down to open the second patent slot. Move the time marker outward one row to unlock new technologies. The leftover die goes back to the black hole. Roll fresh dice for round 2 and repeat.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Redemption
walkthrough.text: In round 2, Blue learns her lesson. She drafts one die from the right of the median and one from the left, balancing her track movement. She uses a medium-value die to fly a short, targeted flight to claim a pulsar, and uses her other die to start a gyrodyne spinning on a previously claimed pulsar. She also has enough cubes this time to copy the leftover die as a bonus action. Much better. Blue is back in the game.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen the complete flow of a round in Pulsar 2849. You watched Blue learn from three common mistakes: drafting aggressively without balancing track costs, wasting a high die on a long unfocused flight, and running out of engineering cubes for the bonus die. Remember: balance your dice draft, fly with purpose, and keep cubes in reserve. You are ready to explore the star cluster on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to technologies, transmitter icons, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun out there in the star cluster!

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players: Red, Green, Blue
Covers a complete first round from dice draft to production
Blue will make classic beginner mistakes and learn from them

summary.title: The Dice Phase Begins
bullets: First player rolls all silver dice and places on matching board spaces
Find the median die and place the marker
Equal dice on both sides = marker stays on that die space

summary.title: Red Drafts First: Grabbing the 6
bullets: Red takes the 6 (3 spaces right of median)
Moves engineering counter 3 spaces right — sacrificing cubes for a powerful die
Strategic trade-off: high die value costs track position

summary.title: Green Drafts: Taking the 3 for Free
bullets: Green takes a die on the median space
Zero track movement — no cost to either counter
Median dice are always tempting because they are free

summary.title: Blue's Mistake: Ignoring the Tracks
bullets: Blue takes two dice both to the right of median (5 and 4)
Both counters pushed right: worse play order AND fewer cubes
Great dice values but terrible track consequences

summary.title: What Blue Did Wrong
bullets: Two high dice = both counters pushed right = penalties
Better approach: pair high die with low or median die
Balance track cost against die value for optimal drafting

summary.title: Finishing the Draft
bullets: Second die draft goes in reverse order
Green and Red take low dice, improving their track positions
Leftover die on board can be copied as bonus die for 4 engineering cubes

summary.title: Red's Turn: Flying and Spinning
bullets: 1 die for short flight: explore system, get blue planet bonus (modifier tokens)
Free gyrodyne placement on claimed pulsar
6 die to start large gyrodyne spinning — points every round from now on

summary.title: Why Red's Turn Was Efficient
bullets: Three accomplishments with two dice: explore, place, spin
Exploration bonus gave resources without costing an extra die
Early spinning gyrodyne compounds points over all remaining rounds

summary.title: Green's Turn: Building Transmitters
bullets: 2 die to take a transmitter tile, covering one die cost
Will finish paying transmitter costs in future rounds
3 die to start a small gyrodyne spinning

summary.title: Blue's Mistake: Wasting a High Die
bullets: Blue spends 5 die on a long flight passing through systems
Passing through = lifeless planet only, no exploration bonus
High die value wasted on distance instead of a targeted action

summary.title: Lesson: Plan Your Flights Carefully
bullets: Short targeted flights often beat long scenic tours
Exploration bonus only when flight ENDS at system with blue planet
Save high dice for actions requiring high numbers (spinning gyrodynes)

summary.title: Blue's Second Mistake: Forgetting the Bonus Die
bullets: Leftover 3 on board costs 4 cubes to copy as bonus die
Blue only has 2 cubes — engineering counter pushed too far right
Aggressive drafting cost both track position AND the bonus action

summary.title: Lesson: Engineering Cubes Are Currency
bullets: 4 cubes = bonus die = effectively a third action per round
Cubes also buy end-game goal bonuses
Leftover cubes score 1 point per pair at game end

summary.title: The Production Phase
bullets: Reorder play by initiative track; hand out cubes by engineering track
Penalty spaces: -1 or -2 points per counter on those spaces
Spinning gyrodynes produce face value + row bonus; Blue scores nothing here

summary.title: Setting Up for Round 2
bullets: Discard untaken transmitters, deal 3 new
Slide first-patent tokens down to open second slots
Advance time marker to unlock new technology row

summary.title: Blue's Redemption
bullets: Blue drafts one high die and one low die, balancing tracks
Short flight to claim pulsar + spin a gyrodyne
Has enough cubes to copy leftover die as bonus action

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have seen a complete round: dice draft, actions, production
Blue's three lessons: balance drafting, fly with purpose, keep cubes in reserve
You are ready to play on your own

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics and pitfalls
Reference the Appendix for technologies, icons, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide via Q&A tab for help anytime during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, drafting two high dice every round and getting crushed on both tracks. Second, flying your ship long distances without a clear objective, wasting high dice as fuel. Third, ignoring engineering cubes and then being unable to copy bonus dice or buy goal bonuses at game end. Fourth, neglecting to get gyrodynes spinning early, missing out on rounds of compounding production points. Fifth, forgetting that goal tiles exist and not building toward them. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most common pitfalls.

walkthrough.title: Balance Your Dice Draft
walkthrough.text: The dice draft is the most important decision each round. You want the best die values, but you also need to manage your initiative and engineering tracks. A great approach is to pair a high die with a low die or a median die. This lets you get the action you need while keeping at least one track in good shape. Pay attention to which track you are moving. If you are already far right on initiative, use engineering for the next rightward move. And never forget: the median die is free. It costs you zero track movement, and sometimes a 3 or 4 is exactly what you need.

walkthrough.title: Get Gyrodynes Spinning Early
walkthrough.text: A spinning gyrodyne produces points every single Production Phase. Getting one spinning in round 1 means it will pay out 8 times. Getting one spinning in round 5 means only 4 payouts. The math is clear: early gyrodynes are worth roughly double compared to late ones. Small gyrodynes are the easiest to spin because they only need a 1 to take and a 3 to flip. Even though large gyrodynes produce more per round, a small gyrodyne spinning from round 1 often outscores a large one that does not start until round 4.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Flights
walkthrough.text: Flying your survey ship is exciting but expensive. Every flight costs you a full die. Make each one count by ending at a location that gives you something valuable, ideally a blue planet with a good exploration bonus or an unclaimed pulsar. Short, targeted flights of 1 to 3 segments are usually better than long sweeping ones. If you need to claim a distant pulsar, check whether a technology can teleport you there instead. And remember, you get the exploration bonus only when your flight ends at a system with a blue planet available, not when you pass through.

walkthrough.title: Engineering Cubes Are More Valuable Than They Look
walkthrough.text: New players often ignore engineering cubes, but they are one of the game's most versatile resources. Four cubes let you copy the leftover die as a bonus action, effectively giving you a third die for the round. Cubes also buy goal tile bonuses at end of game, which can swing 6 to 12 points. And leftover cubes score 1 point per pair. Keep your engineering counter to the left of the track whenever possible. A couple of rounds without cubes can cost you 20 or more points over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Technologies Are Game-Changers
walkthrough.text: Technologies unlock gradually as the time marker advances, and the best ones get patented quickly. Scout the technology boards at the start of the game and plan which ones you want. Some technologies give you ongoing abilities that bend the rules in your favor, like scoring points every time you do a certain action, or letting you use dice one higher or lower than normal. Patent early rows before they are taken by opponents. Each technology can only be patented by two players, so timing matters.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Ignore Goal Tiles
walkthrough.text: The three goal tiles in each game represent a huge chunk of end-game points. Each goal has a base reward plus up to two bonus tiers you can buy with engineering cubes. Fully achieving a goal and buying both bonuses can be worth 18 points or more. At the start of the game, read all three goals and pick one or two that align with your strategy. If a goal rewards spinning gyrodynes, prioritize those. If it rewards stations, fly more aggressively. Do not try to achieve all three unless they naturally overlap with your actions.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Point Sources
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players do not rely on a single strategy. They collect points from gyrodynes, transmitters, technologies, exploration bonuses, goal tiles, initiative position, and stations. You do not need to be the best at everything, but completely ignoring any major category leaves points on the table. A strong core strategy built around gyrodynes or transmitters, supplemented by opportunistic grabs in other categories, tends to outperform a one-dimensional approach. Keep an eye on what is available and grab easy points whenever they present themselves.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't draft two high dice every round — track penalties compound
Don't fly long distances without a clear objective
Don't neglect engineering cubes — they buy bonus dice and goal bonuses
Don't delay spinning gyrodynes — early ones compound over 8 rounds
Don't forget to check and build toward goal tiles

summary.title: Balance Your Dice Draft
bullets: Pair a high die with a low or median die to manage tracks
Alternate which track absorbs rightward movement
Median die = free, zero track cost — often undervalued
Check track positions before drafting to avoid penalties

summary.title: Get Gyrodynes Spinning Early
bullets: Round 1 gyrodyne = 8 production payouts; Round 5 = only 4
Small gyrodynes (cost 1 + 3) are easiest to spin quickly
Early small gyrodyne often outscores late large gyrodyne
Production points compound — prioritize early spinning

summary.title: Plan Your Flights
bullets: Short, targeted flights (1-3 segments) beat long scenic tours
End at blue planet for exploration bonus; passing through gives nothing
Each flight costs a full die — make it count
Check if a technology can teleport you instead of spending a die

summary.title: Engineering Cubes Are More Valuable Than They Look
bullets: 4 cubes = copy leftover die as bonus action (effectively 3rd die)
Cubes buy goal tile bonuses at end of game (6-12 points each)
Leftover cubes = 1 pt per pair at game end
Keep engineering counter left to maximize cube income

summary.title: Technologies Are Game-Changers
bullets: Scout tech boards at game start; plan which ones to patent
Ongoing abilities bend rules in your favor for rest of game
Patent early rows before opponents take them (max 2 per tech)
Green techs score every round; purple techs score at game end

summary.title: Do Not Ignore Goal Tiles
bullets: Each goal: base points + 2 buyable bonus tiers with engineering cubes
Fully achieved goal with bonuses can be worth 18+ points
Pick 1-2 goals that align with your strategy at game start
Save engineering cubes for buying bonuses at game end

summary.title: Diversify Your Point Sources
bullets: Points come from: gyrodynes, transmitters, techs, exploration, goals, initiative, stations
Don't completely ignore any major scoring category
Strong core strategy + opportunistic grabs = highest scores
Watch what's available and grab easy points when they appear

walkthrough.title: The Gyrodyne Rush
walkthrough.text: The gyrodyne rush is one of the strongest openings. Your goal is to claim pulsars and spin gyrodynes as fast as possible. A spinning gyrodyne from round 1 produces points for all 8 rounds. Ideally, claim a pulsar on your first flight, take a small gyrodyne tile cheaply, place it for free, and spin it with a 3 die all in one or two rounds. A small gyrodyne spinning from round 1 produces roughly 40 to 56 points over the game depending on the row bonus values. Add a second gyrodyne by round 3 and you have a production engine that puts you ahead on points without needing to do anything else.

walkthrough.title: Gyrodyne Rush: Advanced Execution
walkthrough.text: To maximize the rush, combine it with construction awards. Get two small gyrodynes on the board as fast as possible to grab the 7-point award before anyone else. Prioritize technologies that make gyrodynes cheaper or let you spin them with a die one higher or lower than normal. The technology that scores 3 points every time you start a gyrodyne spinning is devastating with this strategy. Look for planetary systems that offer free gyrodyne tiles as their exploration bonus, saving you a die.

walkthrough.title: The Transmitter Network
walkthrough.text: The transmitter strategy focuses on building long linked arrays. Each time you complete two linked transmitters, you create a bonus die, essentially getting three actions per round. Online transmitters also produce points or cubes every Production Phase, so a completed array of four or five transmitters is a constant income source. The key is to plan your links before taking transmitters. Look at the half-die symbols on the ends and think about which values you want your bonus dice to be. A bonus die of 5 or 6 is much more valuable than a 1 or 2.

walkthrough.title: Transmitter Network: Timing and Tempo
walkthrough.text: The biggest risk with transmitters is tempo. Each one costs multiple dice to complete, meaning you might spend rounds 1 and 2 paying dice costs with nothing to show for it while other players are spinning gyrodynes and scoring. To mitigate this, prioritize transmitters with only one or two die costs. Complete them quickly to start generating production benefits. And always try to link transmitters so they produce a bonus die, effectively repaying the investment. A single unlinked transmitter with mediocre production is rarely worth the dice you spent on it.

walkthrough.title: The Technology Patent Strategy
walkthrough.text: Technologies can reshape your entire game. A patent-heavy strategy focuses on grabbing the most impactful technologies early, especially those with ongoing abilities. Green technologies that score every round are the most time-sensitive, like gyrodynes. Purple end-game technologies are powerful but patient; they pay off in final scoring. The best patent strategies identify synergistic combinations: a technology that scores points when you fly past jump gates pairs perfectly with a flight-focused approach. Two or three well-chosen patents can add 30 or more points to your final score.

walkthrough.title: The Exploration Strategy
walkthrough.text: An exploration-heavy approach flies aggressively to claim pulsars and establish stations across the board. Station points scale aggressively at the top end: 13 stations is worth 50 points, and each station beyond 13 adds 3 more. If you can reach 15 or 16 stations, that is 56 to 59 points from stations alone. Combine this with technologies or exploration bonus tiles that count as extra stations. End flights at blue planets whenever possible for exploration bonuses. Dead end locations are especially lucrative because they grant an extra exploration tile draw.

walkthrough.title: Managing the Initiative Track
walkthrough.text: The initiative track is often overlooked, but it determines play order and is worth up to 7 points at game end. Going first in the draft means you get the best die, the best transmitter, and first crack at technologies with limited patent slots. Being far left on initiative also protects you from -1 and -2 penalties. The best players manipulate initiative deliberately: they take a low die or a median die specifically to push their initiative counter left when they need to go first the following round for a key technology or transmitter.

walkthrough.title: Engineering Cube Optimization
walkthrough.text: Engineering cubes fund your end game. Every round where you have enough cubes to copy the leftover die, you effectively get a 50 percent increase in actions. Over 8 rounds, that is 8 bonus actions versus zero. Keep your engineering track counter to the left. Spend cubes on bonus dice when the leftover die is useful, but save at least 4 to 6 cubes for end-game goal bonuses. The goal tile bonus structure rewards having cubes: you can buy 6 points for 4 cubes and then another 6 for 1 more cube. That is 12 points for 5 cubes, an incredible rate.

walkthrough.title: Goal Tile Planning
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, read all three goal tiles and identify which ones align with your strategy. Goals that require spinning gyrodynes pair with the gyrodyne rush. Goals that require stations pair with exploration. Goals that require technologies pair with the patent approach. Do not try to achieve all three goals unless they naturally overlap. Instead, focus hard on one or two and fully buy their bonuses with cubes. A single fully achieved goal with both bonuses bought can be worth 18 points. Two fully achieved goals can swing the game by 30 or more points.

walkthrough.title: HQ Board Mastery
walkthrough.text: In standard games, HQ boards add a powerful layer of strategy. The project pyramid rewards concentrated investment: complete the bottom projects quickly to unlock the high-scoring top project. Gate run projects are especially strong. A well-timed gate run on a flight visiting 3 or 4 jump gates of one color can score 9 to 12 points from a single flight. Choose your HQ side based on your overall plan: Side 1 for gyrodyne strategies, Side 2 for flight-heavy strategies. The top project is always worth pursuing if you can complete 4 or 5 lower projects by mid-game.

summary.title: The Gyrodyne Rush
bullets: Claim pulsar early, spin gyrodyne by round 1-2 for max production payouts
Small gyrodyne spinning from round 1 = ~40-56 points over the game
Two early gyrodynes create a point engine that runs all game
Combine with construction awards for 7-11 bonus points

summary.title: Gyrodyne Rush: Advanced Execution
bullets: Race for 2 same-size gyrodynes to grab the 7-point construction award
Patent technologies that reduce gyrodyne costs or score per spin
Look for planetary systems offering free gyrodyne tiles
The 'score 3 per spin start' technology is devastating with this approach

summary.title: The Transmitter Network
bullets: Linked online transmitters create bonus dice = 3 actions per round
Online transmitters produce points or cubes every Production Phase
Plan links before taking transmitters — aim for high-value bonus dice (5-6)
Long arrays of 4-5 transmitters = constant income engine

summary.title: Transmitter Network: Timing and Tempo
bullets: Biggest risk: spending rounds paying costs while opponents score
Prioritize 1-2 die cost transmitters for quick completion
Always link transmitters for bonus dice — unlinked single transmitters are weak
Complete early to start generating production benefits

summary.title: The Technology Patent Strategy
bullets: Green techs score every round (time-sensitive, like gyrodynes)
Purple techs score at game end (patient, high-ceiling)
Look for synergistic combos: flight tech + exploration strategy
2-3 well-chosen patents can add 30+ points to final score

summary.title: The Exploration Strategy
bullets: 13 stations = 50 pts; each beyond 13 = +3 more (aggressive scaling)
End flights at blue planets for exploration bonuses
Dead end locations grant extra exploration tile draws
Technologies/tiles counting as extra stations amplify this strategy

summary.title: Managing the Initiative Track
bullets: First in draft = best die, best transmitter, first patent slot
Far left initiative avoids -1/-2 penalties AND scores 7 pts at game end
Deliberately take low/median dice to push initiative left when needed
Initiative is both tactical (play order) and strategic (end-game points)

Save 4-6 cubes for end-game goal bonuses (12 pts for 5 cubes is elite rate)

summary.title: Goal Tile Planning

Don't spread thin across all 3 unless they naturally overlap

summary.title: HQ Board Mastery
bullets: Complete bottom projects quickly to unlock pyramid top (high scoring)
Gate runs: 3-4 same-color gates visited in one flight = 9-12 points
Side 1 for gyrodyne strategy; Side 2 for flight-heavy strategy
Top project is always worth pursuing if lower projects complete by mid-game

entries.definition: The first phase of each round. The starting player rolls all silver dice, places them on the dice board by value, determines the median, and then players draft two dice each. The first draft goes in play order, the second in reverse order. The leftover die can be copied as a bonus die for 4 engineering cubes.

The second phase of each round. Each player takes one turn in play order, spending their two drafted dice (plus up to one bonus die) on actions such as flying, taking gyrodynes, spinning gyrodynes, building transmitters, patenting technologies, completing HQ projects, or buying die modifiers.

The third phase of each round. Players determine new play order from the initiative track, receive engineering cubes from the engineering track, take track penalties, score online transmitters, score spinning gyrodynes, score green technologies, and set up for the next round.

The middle value of the rolled dice, used as the reference point for the dice draft. Taking a die to the right of the median pushes a tracker right; taking one to the left pushes it left. Taking the die on the median costs zero movement. If more dice are on one side, the marker shifts one space toward that side.

A physical marker placed on the dice board indicating the median value. It can land on a die space (equal dice on both sides) or between two spaces (unequal sides). Used as the reference for all dice draft track movements.
The main dice used for actions. 9 are used in a 4-player game, 7 in a 2- or 3-player game. Rolled at the start of each round, drafted by players, and spent on actions during the Action Phase.

A special die used to represent bonus dice. When a player gains a bonus die, they set the red die to the appropriate value, use it for one action, then return it to the black hole. Only one bonus die can be used per turn.

An extra die available once per turn beyond the two drafted dice. Sources include: copying the leftover die for 4 engineering cubes, linking two online transmitters, certain technologies, and some exploration bonuses. Represented physically by the red die.

A token that can change a die's value by exactly 1, up or down. Each player starts with one. More can be earned from exploration bonuses, technologies, HQ projects, or bought by spending a 1 or 2 die. Spent tokens return to the die modifiers board.

A token that adds 2 to a die's value. Can be bought by spending a 2 die or earned from various game effects. Cannot be combined with other modifier tokens, but can be combined with technology abilities. Spent tokens return to the die modifiers board.
The central area of the dice board. Dice start here at the beginning of each round. Used dice are returned here during the Action Phase to indicate they have been spent.

A track on the dice board tracking play order priority. Players farther left go first in the next round and score more end-game points (7 for 1st, 4 for 2nd, 2 for 3rd). The right end has -1 and -2 penalty spaces that cost points during Production.

A track on the dice board tracking engineering cube income. Players farther left receive more cubes during the Production Phase. The right end has -1 and -2 penalty spaces. Track rewards vary by player count.

Spaces on the dice board where rocket figures are placed to show turn order. Rearranged during each Production Phase based on initiative track position. The first play-order space determines who rolls the dice and drafts first.

The central game board showing pulsars, planetary systems, jump gates, flight segments, and the score track around the edge. Double-sided: one side has dead end locations, the other does not. The side without dead ends is recommended for first games.

Starting locations for survey ships on the star cluster board. Players choose entry gates in reverse play order during setup. Ships fly from entry gates on their first flight and never return to them.

A type of location on the star cluster board. Jump gates connect flight segments and are key to gate run scoring. They come in different colors (orange, purple, etc.) and are visited during flights for gate run points.

A location on the star cluster board that can be claimed by ending a flight there. Once claimed with a ring, no other player can claim or develop it. Pulsars host gyrodynes that produce points when spinning. Some pulsars are isolated (no flight segments) and can only be claimed via special technologies or transmitters.

A pulsar with no flight segments connecting to it. Cannot be reached by flying a survey ship. Can only be claimed through specific technologies or transmitter effects that allow claiming an isolated pulsar.

A location on the star cluster board containing planets. Tiles start face-down and are revealed when a ship visits. Each system has blue planets and possibly lifeless planets. Ending a flight at a system with an available blue planet grants an exploration bonus.

A planet in a planetary system that provides an exploration bonus when a player's flight ends there. If both blue and lifeless planets are available, a player ending their flight must take a blue planet. Passing through allows taking a blue planet only if no lifeless planets remain.

A planet in a planetary system that provides no exploration bonus. When passing through a system, players must place tokens on lifeless planets first if available. No bonus is gained from lifeless planets.

A player's token placed on a planet in a planetary system. Each planet can hold only one token. A player can have at most one station per planetary system. Stations contribute to end-game scoring: 50 points for 13 stations, +3 per station beyond 13.

A location on one side of the star cluster board connected by doubled flight segments. Players can fly out and back along doubled segments in one action. Claiming a dead end pulsar or establishing a station in a dead end system grants an extra exploration bonus tile draw.

A path connecting two locations on the star cluster board. Survey ships move along segments, with each segment costing one point of die value. A segment cannot be retraced within a single flight (one die), but can be reused on separate flights in the same turn.

Each player's rocket figure on the star cluster board. Moves by spending a die equal to the number of segments traveled. Explores planetary systems by flipping tiles, establishes stations at planets, and claims pulsars. Each player has one survey ship.

A reward gained when a player's flight ends at a planetary system with an available blue planet. The player can take the bonus printed on the system tile OR draw a random exploration bonus tile. Bonuses include points, die modifiers, gyrodyne tiles, and more.

Random face-down tiles drawn as an alternative to a system's printed bonus. Most give points and are discarded immediately. Some give die modifiers, engineering cubes, or other benefits. One special tile counts as an extra station at end of game.

A megastructure built on a claimed pulsar. Comes in three sizes: small, medium, and large. Must be placed on a claimed pulsar (free action), then spun by spending a die (3 for small, 5 for medium, 6 for large). Once spinning, it produces points every Production Phase.

The smallest gyrodyne tile. Costs a 1 die to take from the board and a 3 die to start spinning. Produces the fewest points per round but is the easiest and cheapest to deploy. Ideal for early-game rushing.
A mid-sized gyrodyne tile. Costs a 2 die to take from the board and a 5 die to start spinning. Produces moderate points per round. A good balance of cost and output.

The largest gyrodyne tile. Costs a 4 die to take from the board and a 6 die to start spinning. Produces the most points per round but requires high-value dice. Best when spun early enough to justify the investment.

A gyrodyne that has been flipped face-up on a claimed pulsar. It produces points during every Production Phase equal to its face value plus the row bonus indicated by the time marker. Keeps spinning for the rest of the game.

A bonus awarded to players who are first or second to have at least two gyrodynes of the same size on the board (under construction or spinning). First player gets 7 points, second gets 4 points. Awards exist independently for each of the three sizes.
A colored ring placed around a pulsar when a player claims it. Each player has 6 rings, limiting total pulsar claims to 6. Once claimed, no other player can claim or develop that pulsar.

A tile representing part of an energy transmission array. Has die costs that must be paid to complete it. Once all costs are paid, it flips to its online side and provides production benefits. Can be linked to other transmitters to form bonus dice.

A linked chain of transmitter tiles placed end-to-end. Links are formed by matching half-die symbols on tile edges. Once two adjacent linked transmitters are both online, they form a bonus die that can be used that turn.

A transmitter tile that has been fully paid and flipped over. Provides production benefits during every Production Phase (points, engineering cubes, or buyable points). Can form bonus dice when linked to another online transmitter.

The connection between two adjacent transmitter tiles in an array. Formed by half-die symbols on tile edges. When both linked transmitters are online, the two halves form a complete die value that becomes a bonus die for that turn.

One of six double-sided boards showing technologies available for patenting. Three boards are used per game, one from each category (I, II, III). Technologies are arranged in rows unlocked progressively by the time marker.

A marker that advances one row outward on the technology boards at the end of each round. Indicates which technology rows are available for patenting. Also shows the gyrodyne production bonus for the current round.

The act of claiming a technology by spending the required die and placing your token. Each technology can be patented by at most two players. Patenting grants the technology's benefit (immediate, ongoing, or end-game depending on type).
A technology that scores points at the end of every Production Phase after being patented. Green techs are time-sensitive: the earlier you patent them, the more rounds they produce points.

A technology that scores points at the end of the game during final scoring. Purple techs are patient investments; their value does not depend on when they are patented, only on whether their conditions are met at game end.

A resource earned based on engineering track position during the Production Phase. Used to copy leftover dice as bonus actions (4 cubes), buy die modifier tokens, purchase goal tile bonuses at game end, and scored as 1 point per pair at game end.
A token representing 4 engineering cubes. Players can exchange freely between cubes and tokens at any time. Used for convenience when accumulating large cube reserves.

A unique double-sided player board with a pyramid of projects. Not used in first games. Each player gets a random HQ board and chooses a side before the first dice roll. Side 1 favors gyrodynes, Side 2 favors flying.

An action on the HQ board completed by spending the indicated die. Projects are arranged in a pyramid: bottom row available first, upper rows unlock as lower ones complete. Most give immediate benefits; some unlock gate runs.

A special scoring opportunity from certain HQ projects. When flying your survey ship, announce the gate run and score points for each jump gate of the specified color visited during that flight. Each gate run project can be used only once per game. Starting location does not count.

One of six double-sided tiles defining end-of-game scoring goals. Three are used per game. Each goal has a condition to meet, base points, and up to two bonus tiers purchasable with engineering cubes. Goals reward achievements like spinning gyrodynes, stations, patents, or online transmitters.
End-game goal requiring at least 2 or 3 spinning gyrodynes of the same type. If two goal tiles both require spinning gyrodynes, the same gyrodyne cannot count for both goals.

entries.term: Goal: Different Types of Spinning Gyrodynes
entries.definition: End-game goal requiring at least 2 different types of spinning gyrodynes (e.g., one small and one large).
End-game goal requiring at least 3 or 4 online (fully completed and flipped) transmitter tiles.
End-game goal requiring at least 9 or 11 player tokens on planets on the star cluster board. Technologies and exploration tiles counting as extra stations do NOT apply to this goal.
End-game goal requiring at least 5 or 6 technologies patented by the player.
End-game goal requiring at least 5 completed HQ projects. Gate run projects count even if the gate run has not been used.
End-game goal requiring at least 3 completed and used gate run projects. A gate run used on a flight that scored 0 points still counts as used.

End-of-game scoring for total stations in planetary systems. The scoring table on the quick reference sheet scales up to 50 points for 13 stations, with +3 points for each station beyond 13. Some exploration tiles and technologies count as extra stations for this step only.
End-of-game scoring based on final initiative track position. Always 7 points for 1st and 4 points for 2nd. In 2- and 4-player games, 2 points for 3rd. In a 3-player game, no points for 3rd place.
A player-colored tile placed near space 0 at setup. When a player's score reaches 100, the tile is placed face-up. At 200, it is flipped to the 200 side. Helps track scores above 100.

An optional setup variant for the first round only. In a 4-player game, pre-set 6 dice to values 1-6 and roll only 3. In a 2/3-player game, pre-set 4 dice to 2-5 and roll 3. Reduces the impact of extreme rolls in round 1.

In a 2-player game, each player has 2 rocket figures on play-order spaces (choosing 4 dice total, 2 per player in alternating order). Technologies with one die icon can be patented by only one player total. Three dice are left over. Tokens in a nonplayer color block planets labeled with certain icons.
A board placed against the star cluster board holding stacks of gyrodyne tiles (small, medium, large) and construction award tiles. Shows the die cost to take each size of gyrodyne.
A small board placed against the star cluster board holding stacks of plus-minus and plus-two die modifier tokens. Spent tokens are returned here. Also shows the cost to buy modifier tokens.
A player aid card summarizing the round structure, available actions, production phase steps, and end-of-game scoring. Each player should keep one handy during play.
